item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice twist, having the SCP believe that it was once part of its own research project. I do have a sneaking suspicion that these constructed identities aren't entirely fictional, but I enjoyed it either way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I upvoted because I really loved the interview at the end. I really felt sorry for the SCP quite a bit. I'd like to see you write a tale about this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could you give an example?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm very much on the fence with this one, and that's weird because individually I really like several of the parts here. But their sum leaves us with several things unanswered that I feel, in-universe, should be answered.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yup.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, anqxyr, you made your own site, uh?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That poor fucker. It's not just that he's got an entire lifetime of memories in his head, of things he's done, relationships he's been a part of, little victories and defeats that make up a life, that don't jibe with ANYONE elses. It's that, in a month, give or take, they're all just going to wink out of existence like the phosphor dot in a CRT. He doesn't even get the privilege of dying. And he KNOWS it.

Jesus. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Remember that one time? World domination, wasn't it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, even so, I think him being aware makes this all the better, and makes this just good enough for at least one upvote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, he knows that he's 1540. He's been studying it for two years, he realized what he was/wasn't the moment he woke up in the cell and saw the pieces of the cocoon around him. He knows that his wife won't remember him because he knows he's fictional.

A lot of people have a "meh" reaction to the current version, and I kind of understand them. I have some ideas about how to rewrite it to shift the hook from the interview to… other things. But that probably won't happen soon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ok, that does clarify it for me, though I do have some questions still. Like why does "Hawkins" assume/know that his wife won't remember him? Does he know that he's 1540? If not then why does he think he's being contained?

Now that I have some answers, this page definitely doesn't deserve a downvote though I'm not sure it's good enough for me to upvote. Maybe if I read it again a few times it'll seem better but for now I'm neutral. And the way the interview begins still feels odd. Seems like there should be quotation marks around Hawkins or something though I understand why that might not work, but even so it feels too informal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The interviewed is indeed 1540, who, in the current iteration, thinks that he is David Hawkins, a Foundation biologist who has been researching 1540 for the past two years. The reason he's referred to as Hawkins in the transcript is to conceal his identity until the reveal, and also because in-character there needs to be a way to note who the skip thinks he is during different interviews.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm just not getting this. I'm assuming I'm missing something, based on all the praise this has gotten, but I'm just confused by it. Especially concerning the interview. I'm a little put off since there's just an interview log there with nothing explaining why there is, and then I'm really put off by that it explains nothing. Based on the posts referring to "a twist" I can only piece together that the guy they're interviewing is the SCP, but then why is he referred to as Hawkins? Can someone clarify this to me, cause I'd hate to look stupid, but as long as this just confuses me in a bad way, nothing will stop me from downvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me a lot to several other articles, specifically those who erase people's existence from our universe but not the person itself (like that hospital room, and the funny donut on steroids). Oh well, neutral vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man, what a twist. Good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If it were continually getting smaller, I imagine after 20 years it'd be gone already.

Clarified in the description that the mass loss isn't permanent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hm. I swear I remember reading something about a guy who tried to make himself a werewolf, but then ended up in a constant state of transformation, becoming different individuals. Maybe it was just a failed occult thing…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nope. Werewolves are a point of special interest to me, though they've never fared well enough on this site to stick around. The seven furs (SCP-801) include a wolfskin coat, and the comic book (SCP-454) mentions one on the cover, but that's it. No other mentions of werewolves outside the guides and a joke SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was pretty surprising. The interview is giving me a lot to think about, and I like the werewolf angle.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If the intent is to have the SCP slowly wasting away, that's cool. That's the impression I got from the way the line was written. Which, to me, seems like something which might occasion alarm by researchers who would no doubt have noticed the trend and worried about what happens in the inevitable eventuality of it no longer being of enough mass to support itself as a human. The other way to look at it is that the reincarnation is missing some part of its mass, but it's a manageable amount of mass, possibly consisting of fat and muscle mass, and the figure regains it, such as by eating (since it's not ectoentropic, it wouldn't just gain weight, but I suppose it could be pulling mass in from anywhere).

As a reader, I see it as one of two situations. Either there is a consistent reduction in mass, in which case researchers would be speculating on what will happen once it passes a certain point, or the SCP averages a reasonable mass through readding mass which is subsequently lost. Either way, in my opinion (worth the electrons that represent it symbolically) it should be noted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Even if it isn't able to regain the 5-8% it loses, it isn't that big of a deal, because it'll only lose 5-8% of the new weight, which will continue to get smaller and smaller. Doesn't need to regain it all before the next Event.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …Explicitly state that it eats food and drinks water throughout the month? That seems a little excessive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed the entry. I do have to wonder about the bit where it loses 5-8% of his mass each time. Either it's getting smaller each time, or you might want to add a line indicating that it regains said mass somehow, perhaps through increased caloric intake in the weeks after the transformation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one is difficult for me to assess. It's very well executed, and the idea works well, but it doesn't grab me all the way. I feel like there needs to be a little more on the fictional construct/deletion question, either some small inconsistencies (the subject's family having an extra room in their house, open space in pictures, etc. Nothing conclusive, just enough to make us wonder.) or some more interviews (probably before the current one. It feels very stinger-like.) or something.

No vote at the moment, though I'm leaning towards up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not that I'm aware of.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the concept and the implications, but I didn't really feel anything from this.
No vote.

Also, we already have a "failed werewolf transformation attempt" SCP, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey, it's not like all of my demands are unreasonable and unattainable. :U

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ####

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good enough that I read to the end, but overall it felt flat to me.

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with Fortune, having the SCP head their own research was a nice touch.
I did feel it was lacking a bit of censorship. Namely the site locations, names, etc.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvote rescinded.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Loved this. Upvote. Unique, new, creepy. Loved the interview log. I like the implication in the above post that this thing is actually deleting individuals. Also great, competent writing especially since you needed no [REDACTED] to still make us ask questions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm still highly amused by the reference to Provo, Utah.

Downvoting because of specific humanoid cell measurements in the Procedures block. Minor but highly annoying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What got my vote was realizing that Dr. Hawkins is SCP-1540 thinking that it has spent time as the head of its own research project.

It's subtle, and gives me shivers. +1.

e: Ohnoit'sgotmetooohgodohgodohgod!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neutral voted due to being competently written but just not very interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, alot. And I have a sneaking suspicion that we're slightly wrong about the anomaly. It becomes different people, but I don't think they're fictional. Something during the metamorphosis deletes memory and records of the individual, and we'll never be able to know it because, well, we can't. Memories, ect are changed retroactively. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Enh. It's not bad, but the article as a whole feels like a cross between SCP-200 and that "random metamorphosis" one. And that interview at the end feels like it's trying too hard.

No vote.
